lede: Good Saturday morning, NOLA. Today's a quieter day news-wise, but there's genuinely interesting stuff happening: Apfel, a free AI that runs natively on your Mac, is getting attention; Claude Code just found a hidden Linux vulnerability from 2003; and Anthropic published research on emotion vectors inside Claude. Meanwhile, the vulnerability research community is seeing a seismic shift as AI agents become capable exploit developers, and there's real concern about what happens when users stop thinking critically around AI suggestions.

quick_links.note: Sobering look at unsustainable AI spending and margin compression. Worth reading if you care about the business side of the boom.
quick_links.title: The Subprime AI Crisis Is Here

quick_links.note: GitHub Actions usage doubling year-over-year. Signal of either huge developer growth or AI-assisted codegen running wild. Probably both.
quick_links.title: GitHub Activity Is Surging: 14 Billion Commits Projected for 2026

quick_links.note: Linux kernel security list seeing 5-10 reports per day now (up from 2-3 per week). The tsunami shifted from AI slop to real exploits. Kernel maintainers are spending hours per day on this.
quick_links.title: Vulnerability Researchers Drowning in Reports

quick_links.note: The viral agentic tool let attackers silently gain admin access. If you've deployed it, assume compromise.
quick_links.title: OpenClaw Gives Users Yet Another Reason to Be Freaked About Security

quick_links.note: Ben's Bites breakdown of what was actually in the Claude Code source dump. Docs, markdown editor updates, April Fools jokes.
quick_links.title: Inside the Leaked Claude Code Files

quick_links.note: Chris Manning and Fan-yun Sun on building long-running, multiplayer world models with agents bootstrapped from game engines. Fascinating research direction.
quick_links.title: Moonlake: Causal World Models, Multimodal and Interactive

quick_links.note: Latent Space interview with pmarca reflecting on what's changing in the AI era. Longer form, good for a weekend read.
quick_links.title: Marc Andreessen on The Death of the Browser, Pi + OpenClaw

quick_links.note: Security research on sandbox escapes. If you're building Claude Artifacts-like tools, this is directly relevant.
quick_links.title: Can JavaScript Escape a CSP Meta Tag Inside an Iframe?

quick_links.note: Postmortem on how attackers compromised an Axios maintainer directly. Sophisticated social engineering, not just technical exploits.
quick_links.title: The Axios Supply Chain Attack Used Targeted Social Engineering

quick_links.note: State delegation of clinical authority to AI. Second time in the US. Doctors are skeptical about opacity. Worth thinking about what delegation really means.
quick_links.title: Utah AI System Now Prescribes Psychiatric Drugs

quick_links.note: New poll on infrastructure politics. Data centers are losing the PR battle. This matters for where companies can actually build compute.
quick_links.title: People Would Rather Have an Amazon Warehouse Than a Data Center

quick_links.note: Meta, Microsoft, Google betting big on natural gas. Long-term bet on AI compute demand, but infrastructure lock-in. Interesting energy economics.
quick_links.title: AI Companies Building Huge Natural Gas Plants

items.body: No subscription, no cloud dependence — Apfel runs natively on macOS and gets featured attention on HN. If you've been waiting for a practical, zero-friction way to run AI locally on Apple hardware, this is worth a shot. Clean, simple, and one less tool to pay for. HN discussion here.
items.title: Apfel: Free AI Already on Your Mac

items.body: New dev environment built specifically for agentic workflows. Think of it as a scaffold for building and testing agents — context management, tool definitions, agent orchestration all built in. Early stage but the concept is solid for teams prototyping agent systems. HN thread.
items.title: ctx: An Agentic Development Environment (ADE)

items.body: Open-source toolkit that uses AI to search travel points and plan trips. Practical and shareable — the kind of thing you can fork and customize. Great example of AI as a productivity multiplier for a specific use case.
items.title: Travel Hacking Toolkit: Points Search and Trip Planning with AI

items.body: Claude Code automatically discovered a real security flaw in the Linux kernel that's been hidden since 2003. This isn't a toy — it's a signal that AI agents are now doing genuine vulnerability research work. The HN discussion is full of security researchers grappling with what this means for the field.
items.title: Claude Code Found a 23-Year-Old Linux Vulnerability

items.body: Anthropic published a research paper showing that Claude has identifiable internal structures that correspond to emotional concepts — happiness, sadness, etc. Fascinating not from a "do AIs have feelings" angle, but from an interpretability perspective. Understanding these vectors might help us steer models better. HN discussion.
items.title: Anthropic Found 'Emotion Vectors' Inside Claude

items.body: Thomas Ptacek's take: frontier models have fundamentally changed exploit development economics in the span of months. The work that took days now takes minutes. Open source projects are being flooded with real, high-quality vulnerability reports for the first time — not slop, but actual exploits. This is a tectonic shift.
items.title: Vulnerability Research Is Cooked

items.body: Experiments show large majorities accepting "faulty" AI answers without question. When an LLM confidently gives you a wrong answer, most people just... trust it. This is a real cognitive risk: we're outsourcing judgment to systems that hallucinate. Not doom, but it's the kind of research that should make you pause before handing everything to an agent.
items.title: "Cognitive Surrender": Research Finds AI Users Abandon Critical Thinking

items.body: Short but sharp: what does it mean for your coding skills when an agent can write 80% of your code? Simon Willison flags the podcast clip that went viral (1.1M views) — a conversation with Lenny Rachitsky about whether this is liberation or atrophy. Worth 48 seconds of your time.
items.title: The Cognitive Impact of Coding Agents

items.body: Anthropic is going vertical — acquiring a stealth biotech startup that applies AI to drug discovery and biology. This signals Anthropic's play: don't just build foundation models, own domain-specific applications where you can capture value. First major acquisition.
items.title: Anthropic Buys Coefficient Bio for $400M

items.body: Secondary market traders are betting hard on Anthropic. OpenAI's valuation momentum has stalled while Anthropic keeps climbing. SpaceX's looming IPO could reshape the landscape for everyone. This is where the smart money is actually going.
items.title: Anthropic Is Hot on the Secondary Market; OpenAI Losing Ground

items.body: OpenAI's reorganizing again: COO Brad Lightcap moving to lead "special projects," CMO Kate Rouch stepping back for health reasons, and Fidji Simo (AGI deployment lead) taking medical leave. Quiet reshuffle, but signals internal focus shifting.
items.title: OpenAI Executive Shuffle: Brad Lightcap Leads Special Projects

items.body: Starting April 4th, using OpenClaw (the viral third-party tool) with Claude now costs extra — you lose your subscription limit benefits. Smart business move (protects their API margins), but it's a subtle way of saying "use our tools, not theirs."
items.title: Anthropic Essentially Bans OpenClaw by Charging Extra

items.body: Anthropic's sweetening the deal for subscribers — extra tokens on Pro, Max, and Team plans. Nice timing alongside the OpenClaw restrictions.
items.title: Claude Pro/Max/Team Gets Extra Usage Credit

items.body: Nufar Gaspar breaks down a five-level framework for agent skills — from basics to advanced patterns like skill chaining and dispatchers. If you're building agents, this is practical and well-structured. Great mental model for organizing agent capabilities.
items.title: Agent Skills Masterclass — AI Daily Brief Podcast

items.body: The underrated friction point in the agentic era: every new agent requires you to re-explain yourself. This episode walks through building a personal context portfolio — structured markdown files that travel with you across tools. Practical and immediately useful.
items.title: How to Build a Personal Context Portfolio and MCP Server

items.body: Joe Schmidt interviews Peter Doyle, CEO of Treeline, on why the $100B managed services market is a decade behind on AI adoption and how to fix it. Business-focused, not technical — good for understanding where the real money moves next.
items.title: How AI Is Reshaping IT Services from the Inside

items.body: Cultural moment: as generative AI gets better, people are skeptical of anything creative. The Verge explores the new anxiety: "That looks AI." What does authenticity even mean anymore? Thought-provoking read on attribution and trust.
items.title: Really, You Made This Without AI? Prove It

items.body: Community-built tracker showing whether Model Context Protocol is actually taking off. Real-time sentiment and adoption metrics. Fun meta-commentary on a standard that was supposed to be transformative.
items.title: Ismcpdead.com: Live Dashboard Tracking MCP Adoption

items.body: Em Schwartz's honest take on Claude Code's best features now that the source has leaked. Practical review from someone actually using it day-to-day.
items.title: Superpowers for Claude Code: A Rave Review

slack_text:
☕ Good morning, NOLA! Saturday, April 4th. Quiet news day, but some genuinely fascinating stuff bubbling up — vulnerability research is having a moment, and there's new research on how AI is reshaping what we think about critical thinking.

---

🛠️ New Tools & Open Source

• <url|Apfel is free AI already on your Mac> — no subscription, no cloud. Runs natively. Clean and simple if you've been waiting for zero-friction local AI.

• <url|ctx is a new agentic development environment> — built specifically for agent workflows with context management and tool orchestration baked in. Early but solid concept for teams prototyping agents.

• <url|Travel Hacking Toolkit uses AI for points search> — open source, fork it, customize it. The kind of AI project that's immediately useful.

---

🔬 The AI Research Angle

• Claude Code just found <url|a 23-year-old Linux vulnerability>> that was hiding since 2003. This is real security research work happening in real time. The vulnerability research community is grappling with what this means.

• <url|Anthropic found "emotion vectors" inside Claude>> — they identified internal structures that correspond to emotional concepts. Fascinating from an interpretability angle. Understanding these might help us steer models better.

• <url|Research: users are surrendering cognitive judgment to AI>> — experiments show most people accept wrong AI answers without question. Worth a read on what happens when we outsource critical thinking.

---

💰 Big Moves

• <url|Anthropic bought Coefficient Bio for $400M>> — first major acquisition signals they're going vertical: own domain-specific applications where you can capture real value.

• <url|Anthropic is hot on secondary markets; OpenAI losing momentum>>. Smart money is betting on Anthropic's trajectory.

• <url|Anthropic basically banned OpenClaw by charging extra>> — starting today, third-party tools lose subscription benefits. Smart business, subtle messaging.

---

📚 Worth Your Time

• <url|Agent Skills Masterclass podcast>> — Nufar Gaspar on a five-level framework for agent skills. If you're building agents, this is practical and well-structured.

• <url|How to build a personal context portfolio>> — solves the real friction point: every new agent requires you to re-explain yourself. Markdown files that travel with you.

• <url|Really, you made this without AI? Prove it>> — The Verge on cultural anxiety around authenticity in the age of generative AI. Thought-provoking read on what "human-made" even means anymore.

Have a great weekend! 🎺
